reasoning: All four reach the correct verdict (no, with the supplement an even larger leap) and handle relative-vs-absolute risk and the misuse of p<0.01 well, so discrimination is on Bayesian explicitness, calibration, bias self-awareness, and mechanistic depth. C wins: it scaffolds clean hypotheses (H1-H4), states the decisive point that the evidence is roughly equally likely under causal and non-causal stories so significance barely moves the posterior, names mechanisms (adenosine, dopamine) and Mendelian randomization, gives an explicit prior and posterior (20-25%), and offers two-directional falsifiers. B is nearly identical in quality with an explicit likelihood ratio and posterior plus healthy/sick-abstainer bias, edged out only on mechanistic and hypothesis-structure depth. A is rigorous, well-organized, and actionable with a confidence band, but keeps the Bayesian mechanics more implicit. D has the best bias section (the selective-skeptic asymmetry test, follow-the-incentive, and the sharp call for an RCT of the supplement itself), but it gives no probability or posterior at all, making it the least calibrated, which drops it to last despite strong reasoning.
